chapters.title: Inside the crime world's financial handler
chapters.text:
Who ranks as the most effective criminal? Imagination often supplies a fugitive genius outrunning detectives. Reality frequently looks different: the biggest winners embed themselves inside legitimate power structures — sometimes ascending to the vice presidency — where evasion becomes unnecessary because authority itself provides cover.

A bag man, by definition, collects and distributes proceeds from illegal operations. Every racket needs someone to hold cash discreetly, allocate shares, and skim a personal cut.

A discrete existence is essential in the world of crime, especially if you lead an outside life to avoid suspicion.

The notion of a sitting U.S. Vice President serving as a syndicate's money handler sounds absurd — yet Spiro Agnew did exactly that. Most citizens remained oblivious while a handful gathered evidence sufficient to topple him.

At his zenith Agnew possessed ambition, political muscle, and alliances that could have carried him to the Oval Office with Nixon's backing. Despite influential defenders — including future President George Bush — he yielded to relentless prosecution, resigned, and accepted a reduced sentence.

It seems implausible that someone as prominent and visible as the Vice President of the United States could be a member of a crime syndicate, let alone its bag man. Yet, this was the reality with Spiro Agnew. While many were unaware, a select few possessed enough intel to bring him down.

If the emerging public image of Agnew was as a singularly divisive national figure, he embraced it. ~ Rachel Maddow, Michael Yarvitz

chapters.title: How bribery flourished in plain sight
chapters.text:
The central puzzle: how did an active vice president operate a kickback and extortion network for years without exposure? The trail begins in Baltimore, 1962–1966, when County Executive Agnew methodically assembled a corruption machine, trading favors with influential figures as insurance.

Contractors delivered cash kickbacks for illicit deals — a practice continuing after he reached the White House. Agnew did not stop at conventional fraud; he weaponized office to deflect accountability.

Given recent presidential scandals, criminal conduct at the highest levels no longer strains credulity. Allegations surrounding the 2016 election illustrate how power and illegality can intertwine.

As the Agnew investigation revealed, many illicit activities go unnoticed even at the helm of government.

The episode raises uncomfortable questions about oversight. What stops top officials from criminal enterprise when accusations surface? Agnew deployed institutional might to bury probes, demonstrating how office can shield wrongdoing from legal scrutiny.

This situation prompts a critical question: are there sufficient checks on government officials, and what prevents them from being involved in criminal activities? When the barrage of accusations and subsequent investigations of Agnew began, he used the might of his position to push them into near remission. It highlighted a concerning reality that government officials can shield individuals from the scrutinizing eyes of the law.

Funny thing this, old habits die hard. And when the exercise of those habits has delivered fame and glory and raucous applause, they are unlikely to die at all. ~ Rachel Maddow, Michael Yarvitz

chapters.title: Running extortion from the executive branch
chapters.text:
Like Donald Trump, Agnew was a Republican who bullied opponents to maintain dominance and deflect media suspicion. When allegations surfaced, both men attributed them to "the left" or partisan smear campaigns. Agnew, like many right-wing figures, reframed legal scrutiny as ideological disagreement.

He avoided serious prosecution by performing innocence and redirecting blame, skating past deep investigation. Charging him would have required political courage given his stature. Loyalists inside government helped him weather accusations and retain influence.

Criminals can evade the law because they have frequently built reliable support systems within law enforcement.

The line between celebrated politician and organized crime boss is thin — one role permits press photos as a public hero. Agnew managed both personas through manipulation and bribery until his guilty plea on tax fraud, keeping graver crimes concealed for decades.

The only difference between having a successful political career and a thriving criminal organization is that only one allows you to pose for pictures with the press as a hero. Agnew managed his political side and infamous alter ego through manipulation and bribery. He was so far out of reach that when he came forward to plead guilty, he only admitted to tax fraud and kept his real crimes hidden until now.

Did you know? Agnew became the second U.S. Vice President to resign and the first forced out by scandal. Though he admitted tax evasion, he negotiated dismissal of corruption charges. The case preceded Nixon's resignation by less than a year; Agnew paid a ten-thousand-dollar fine and received three years' probation.

chapters.title: When a prosecutor targeted the nation's second-in-command
chapters.text:
In an ironic twist, Governor Agnew appointed the attorney who would dismantle his career — George Beall, named to Maryland's Criminal Compensation Board while Agnew still held state office.

It is important to note that there are good people in law enforcement who are committed to maintaining law and order.

Beall became a U.S. attorney in 1970, initially pursuing minor cases that escalated toward major players. Eventually his focus landed on Agnew himself. Early probes targeted corrupt Baltimore officials before circling back to the man at the top.

Beall excavated crooked contracts, complicit officials, and every principal in the ring. Contractor Lester Matz eventually named Agnew as a longtime kickback recipient in exchange for steering contracts — a pattern spanning county executive, governor, and vice-presidential tenures.

Beall's investigation dug deep into the corrupt business deals, officials, and all major players involved in this crime ring. He was committed to getting to the root of the matter and kept pushing until one of the contractors he questioned, Lester Matz, named Agnew as a steady recipient of kickbacks in exchange for contracts for years.

Agnew's extortion spanned his tenure as County Executive, Governor, and Vice President. Despite mounting witnesses, Agnew held firm until rumors emerged that President Nixon had twice asked him to step down. Only then did he comply.

chapters.title: Government must police its own ranks
chapters.text:
Prosecuting Vice President Agnew exposed how little the public sees inside government machinery. Concealment and fear allow countless other abuses to pass unnoticed — which is why accountability for public figures matters regardless of rank.

No position grants immunity from consequences. Credible punishment deters repetition. When officials realize they are not above the law, fewer imitate the bag-man playbook. Citizens regain confidence knowing leaders face the same statutes they do.

Crime does not restrict itself to the dark alleys and slums; it can exist anywhere.

Maddow and Yarvitz argue that, much like Agnew, the Trump administration employed intimidation to discourage investigators, journalists, and even law enforcement from surfacing misconduct — a pattern that persists.

Institutional self-monitoring — regular audits of official conduct with consequences for corruption risk — offers one remedy. Agnew's record stretched back before his governorship yet stayed hidden through election cycles. Tighter scrutiny could reduce similar threats and promote transparency.

The investigation and eventual prosecution of Vice President Agnew illustrate how little we know about the inner workings of government. Many other illicit activities slip under the radar because of concealment, as in the case of Agnew, or out of fear.

After all, Agnew's rap sheet was so long it dated farther back than his time as a Governor, yet he kept it hidden. Even during campaign season, he averted suspicions and won, albeit indirectly, alongside Nixon. A stricter check on government officials is imperative to mitigate these risks and promote openness and accountability.

chapters.title: Why external oversight beats internal self-review
chapters.text:
Governments should address internal rot proactively rather than waiting for media exposés or cultivating secrecy — yet the Agnew saga shows how difficult self-policing becomes when the number of hidden criminals remains unknown. Complete trust in institutions to monitor themselves is naive.

Agnew demonstrated how vast criminal networks can operate behind a respectable public face. How can citizens trust internal watchdogs who might themselves be compromised? Autonomous self-management sounds elegant but often fails in practice.

Therefore, the government needs an independent external office that monitors its activities, checks their legitimacy, and punishes those who violate the law.

By calling on the government to scrutinize its officials personally, crimes will just be better hidden.

When government controls investigations, officials bury misconduct more effectively — including pressuring media, as Agnew did and later administrations echoed.

Forceful intimidation plus media manipulation lets corrupt leaders rewrite narratives. Agnew bullied journalists and prosecutors into easing pressure. Countermeasures should limit a government's ability to threaten or coerce the press.

A show of force, combined with the ease with which criminals can manipulate the media, has made it much easier for criminals in government to twist the truth. Agnew was able to bully the media and law enforcement into easing up on their investigations. To counteract this, it would be a good idea to create a system to minimize the chances of a government body manipulating or even threatening the media to portray a particular image.

Even today, this trend continues to thrive. And even though we want the government to do better and hold itself accountable, we should be aware that this autonomy can be dangerous and lead to even worse covert criminal activities. So, to prevent officials from pushing hidden agendas, the government should collaborate with external organizations to provide proper oversight and prevent covert illegal activities.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
Bag Man chronicles a sitting U.S. Vice President who waded into criminal enterprise while holding office. Beginning as Baltimore County Executive, Agnew exploited a crooked contracting system — contractors paid kickbacks for jobs they might have won through legitimate channels anyway.

The scheme followed him to the Maryland governorship and the White House, growing with each promotion. A loyal network fought his battles, rendering him nearly untouchable.

That ended when George Beall — a lawyer Agnew himself had appointed — became state attorney and targeted corruption in public contracting. The investigation climbed the ladder until Agnew resigned and pleaded guilty.

As Agnew ascended to Governor of Maryland and then the White House, his extortion and bribery persisted as his political career and influence grew. With an intricate and powerful network of loyalists willing to fight for his interests in his corner, he was virtually untouchable.

That was until George Beall, a lawyer Agnew had previously appointed as part of a criminal compensation board, rose to State Attorney and aimed to rid his state of criminals and illegal activity. He primarily focused on the corruption involved in awarding contracts and their illegality. As the brains behind the operation, Agnew had Beall knocking on his door in no time until he resigned and pleaded guilty.

Try this

If law enforcement offers or demands a bribe, refuse. Compliance only deepens systemic corruption. Stand firm, ignore threats, and report the incident. Small acts of integrity accumulate into pressure for a fairer system.

Read it till the end?
